title: podmanimage: Enable podman.sock
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**


/kind feature

**Description**

It would be nice if the podman docker image had `podman.sock` enabled by default. It would then be possible to just start the container with `/sbin/init` and mount `/var/run/podman` to make `podman.sock` externally available.

comments.body:
The podman image (No need for docker adjective) does not include systemd.  So I am not sure it is worth adding the overhead to add systemd, perhaps we could add a podman-init image along with Podman which would do what you want.

You do not need systemd within the container to do what you want though, try:

$ podman run --privileged -v /run:/run quay.io/podman/stable podman system service -t 0

> No need for docker adjective

good catch ;-)

> The podman image does not include systemd

I tested this previously and it seemed to be available in the image, so I guess it's in the base image. But anyway, your solution is indeed much better, thanks!

Maybe this information could be added to the article which actually caused my interest in exporting the socket from a container: https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/podman-inside-container ? Great article by the way and great work on podman, I'm surprised by cool new features all the time :-)

Unfortunately, further testing shows that for some use cases, running systemd in the image seems to be necessary. I want to use the podman-in-podman setup as a backend for drone-ci. Using the `podman system service -t 0` command as suggested causes this error when trying to run images in drone-ci:
> default: Error response from daemon: writing file `/sys/fs/cgroup/libpod_parent/libpod-9565c84bedfdb07cbd84060ee9ec53e8c839500ff237993746706f5ef25664c8/cgroup.procs`: Operation not supported: OCI runtime error

It works perfectly fine when I use "/sbin/init" as entry point for the container and run `systemctl enable podman.socket` once. I think there's no real drawback from adding `systemctl enable podman.socket` in the image Dockerfile, as it won't have any effect as long as users are not explicitly starting `/sbin/init`. What do you think?

Try to build a container use this Containerfile

```
cat > Containerfile << _EOF
from quay.io/podman/stable
run dnf -y install systemd; dnf -y clean all;
run systemctl enable podman.socket; systemctl disable console-getty systemd-logind; mkdir -p /var/lib/systemd/linger; touch /var/lib/systemd/linger/podman
run su podman -c "systemctl --user enable podman.socket"
entrypoint [ "/sbin/init" ]
_EOF
```
Now Build it
```
podman build -t podman-init .
```
And try it out.

Seems to work for me :+1: (I've only tested root-mode podman). Would be great to have this as an official image.

> systemctl disable console-getty systemd-logind

Didn't have an effect for me if the intention was to disable the login console. `mask` instead of `disable` deactivated it properly.

Great to know, I was fooling around with trying to disable them.  But lost time (and interest)...
Glad you were successful.
